summary:
The 'Black Ships' (Kurofune) refers specifically to the four American naval vessels commanded by Commodore Matthew Perry that arrived in Edo Bay on 8 July 1853 — two steam-powered paddle frigates (the Susquehanna and the Mississippi) and two sailing sloops — whose appearance caused tremendous shock in Japan. The phrase 'Black Ships' and the event they symbolize have become the defining metaphor in Japanese historical consciousness for the sudden arrival of overwhelming foreign power and its demand for change.

The ships' blackness came from their iron hulls and the coal smoke from their steam engines. Their ability to move independently of wind, to sail upwind into harbor, and to carry massive armament made them utterly unlike anything Japan's coastal defenses had been designed to resist. The shogunate's coastal batteries were powerless to stop them. Contemporary Japanese observers wrote of their shock at the strange, smoke-belching vessels and the authority of their commander, who refused to be deflected to the designated port of Nagasaki and presented his letter from President Fillmore directly at Edo, effectively at the gates of the capital.

Perry departed after presenting his demands, announcing he would return the following year for Japan's answer. The [[event:2589|Perry Expedition's]] challenge triggered an intense national debate within Japan. The shogunate's consultation of the feudal lords (daimyo) for advice exposed fundamental disagreements and weakness at the center. The event is widely regarded as the decisive catalyst for the political crisis that led to the [[event:2588|fall of the Tokugawa Shogunate]] and the [[event:2544|Meiji Restoration]].
